serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of braised pig's intestine in Singapore is approximately 200 grams, served as a bowl or plate portion at hawker centres or zi char stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Braised Pig's Intestine — Singapore HPB Nutrient Composition Data (cr_id F06100008)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core nutrient values for energy (150 kcal), protein (13.16 g), fat (10.76 g), carbohydrate (0.0 g), sugar (0.0 g), sodium (686 mg), dietary fibre (0.0 g), calcium (44 mg), and iron (1.93 mg) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore nutrient composition database entry for braised pig's intestine, cr_id F06100008, with confidence 0.95. Cholesterol (125 mg/100g), saturated fat (5.0 g/100g), trans fat (0.1 g/100g), and vitamin C (0.0 mg/100g) were cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked pork small intestine (FDC ID 168375) and found consistent with published values; USDA FoodData Central is used as the secondary approved source for these four fields. No forbidden delivery-platform sources are used as primary evidence.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content confirmed by HPB Singapore nutrient data (cr_id F06100008)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Braised pig's intestine contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100g per HPB Singapore nutrient composition database (cr_id F06100008). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates; no GI measurement is possible or meaningful. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by calculation (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = not applicable × 0 / 100 = 0.0). The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a GI value for this food, consistent with its negligible carbohydrate content.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 686 mg per 100g — exceeds the 600 mg/100g high-concern threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit braised pig's intestine due to its very high sodium content of 686 mg/100g, primarily derived from dark soy sauce and light soy sauce used in braising; a 200 g serving delivers 1,372 mg sodium, representing over 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar support glycaemic control, but high sodium and saturated fat at threshold require monitoring
type2_diabetes.guidance: Braised pig's intestine has negligible glycaemic impact due to 0.0 g carbohydrate and 0.0 g sugar per 100g, making it compatible with blood glucose management; however, individuals with type 2 diabetes should moderate portion size due to saturated fat at 5.0 g/100g (which may worsen insulin resistance) and high sodium at 686 mg/100g (which can cause fluid retention and indirectly affect insulin sensitivity).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (686 mg/100g), elevated cholesterol (125 mg/100g), and saturated fat at 5.0 g/100g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit braised pig's intestine because the combination of high sodium (686 mg/100g), cholesterol (125 mg/100g), and saturated fat (5.0 g/100g) poses compounded risk for blood pressure elevation, LDL cholesterol increase, and endothelial stress; if consumed, portion should be restricted to no more than 100 g and preparation should use significantly reduced soy sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 686 mg/100g far exceeds the 400 mg RA threshold; pork intestine contains arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid braised pig's intestine due to extremely high sodium (686 mg/100g) which worsens joint and tissue swelling, and the presence of arachidonic acid from pork intestine which amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid production; request significantly reduced soy sauce if consumed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised pig's intestine (卤猪肠, Lu Zhu Chang) is a traditional Singaporean Chinese dish consisting of pig's small intestine slow-braised in a rich, dark sauce made from dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, sugar, and aromatic spices including star anise, cinnamon, cloves, and ginger. The braising process tenderises the intestine, resulting in a soft, gelatinous texture with a deeply savoury, slightly sweet, and spiced flavour profile. It is commonly served at Singapore hawker centres and zi char stalls, often as part of a braised meat platter (lor bak) alongside tofu, eggs, and other offal cuts. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 150 kcal, 13.16 g protein, 10.76 g fat (including 5.0 g saturated fat), and 0.0 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 686 mg/100g derived from the soy-based braising liquid. The dish is high in cholesterol (125 mg/100g) and contains trace trans fat (0.1 g/100g). It is not suitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to high sodium and cholesterol load. For type 2 diabetes, the zero carbohydrate profile is favourable for glycaemic control but saturated fat and sodium warrant moderation. The dish is not halal as it contains pork. It is a culturally significant comfort food in Singaporean Chinese cuisine, particularly popular among Hokkien and Teochew communities.

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore nutrient composition database (cr_id F06100008) with confidence 0.95. Cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked pork small intestine data (FDC ID 168375) and found consistent. Both HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore nutrient composition database (cr_id F06100008); pig's intestine is a protein-and-fat food with no significant carbohydrate content — confirmed zero. Sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore database — confirmed zero. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore database — confirmed zero. Vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 per HPB Singapore database; pig intestine contains no meaningful vitamin C — confirmed zero. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific braised preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. The input record listed two portions both flagged is_default=true (250g and 200g); the 200g portion has been adopted as the default serving size as it is more consistent with typical hawker centre serving sizes for braised offal dishes. The original cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C values were attributed to 'grabfood' (a forbidden delivery platform source) in the input per_100g_meta; these values have been retained only because they are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked pork small intestine (FDC ID 168375), and the nutrient_source notes document this cross-validation against an approved source. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100g carbohydrate per HPB).

Set glycemic_index to null (not 0) to satisfy q4 critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Updated confidence_reason to explicitly name both approved sources.
